merged_events.event_description: The American Legation in Mexico City was established when Joel Robert Poinsett presented his credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary.

evidence.quote: The American Legation in Mexico was established on June 1, 1825, when Joel Robert Poinsett presented his credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to President Guadalupe Victoria.

merged_events.event_description: Mexico severed diplomatic relations with the United States in response to U.S. moves toward annexing Texas, handing U.S. Minister Wilson Shannon his dismissal.

evidence.quote: the Mexican Minister of Foreign Affairs informed U.S. Minister to Mexico, Wilson Shannon, on March 28, 1845, that Mexico was severing diplomatic relations with the United States.

merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations were reestablished following the Treaty of Guadalupe-Hidalgo, restoring the U.S. Legation in Mexico.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations were reestablished on December 4, 1848, when U.S. President James K. Polk accepted Luis de la Rosa's credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to the United States.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. suspended and then severed relations with Mexico's conservative government; Envoy Forsyth was recalled by President Buchanan.

evidence.quote: Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to Mexico John Forsyth "suspended" relations, on June 21, 1858, between the legation and the conservative government of Miguel Miramón y Tarelo... Buchanan assented and recalled Forsyth on July 15, 1858.

merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations were reestablished when Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary Robert M. McLane presented his credentials to President Benito Juárez.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations were reestablished on April 6, 1859, when Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to Mexico, Robert M. McLane, presented his credentials to President Benito Juárez.

merged_events.event_description: The American Legation in Mexico City was elevated to Embassy status when Powell Clayton presented his credentials as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary.

evidence.quote: The Legation in Mexico was raised to Embassy status on January 3, 1899, when Powell Clayton presented his credentials as Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary to President Porfirio Díaz.

merged_events.event_description: Mexico severed diplomatic relations with the United States following the U.S. occupation of Veracruz, handing Chargé d'Affaires O'Shaughnessy his passport and requesting his departure.

evidence.quote: Mexican officials severed diplomatic relations with the United States on April 22, when they handed Chargé d'Affaires ad interim O'Shaughnessy his passport and requested his departure from Mexico.

merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations were reestablished at the embassy level when U.S. Ambassador Henry P. Fletcher presented his credentials to President Carranza.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations were reestablished on March 3, 1917, when U.S. Ambassador Henry P. Fletcher presented his credentials to President Carranza.

merged_events.event_description: Nathan Clifford presents credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary, restoring the U.S. Legation in Mexico after the break in relations.

evidence.quote: Nathan Clifford, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1848-07-28. Credentials presented: 1848-10-02. Presented recall on 1849-09-06.

merged_events.event_description: Envoy Forsyth suspends political relations of the U.S. Legation with the Government of Mexico.

evidence.quote: John2 Forsyth, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1856-07-21. Credentials presented: 1856-10-23. Suspended political relations of the Legation with Government of Mexico on 1858-06-21.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. Legation in Mexico is elevated to an Embassy when Powell Clayton is promoted to Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary.

evidence.quote: Powell Clayton, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1897-03-22. Credentials presented: 1897-05-13. Promoted to Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary 1899-01-13.

matched.notes: Exact match on date and status.
Exact match on date and status.

CSV date 1858-06-21 matches the suspension date in both RDCR (Event 3) and POCOM (Event 9). RDCR Event 3 has a citation error flag but the quoted text explicitly mentions June 21, 1858 as the suspension date, consistent with POCOM. The RDCR recall date of July 15 is a separate personnel event (Forsyth's recall by Buchanan).
Exact match on date and status.

CSV date 1899-01-03 matches RDCR (Event 5), which gives the explicit institutional date when Clayton presented credentials as Ambassador. POCOM (Event 10) gives 1899-01-13 as the promotion date, which is a personnel event. CSV correctly follows RDCR per source priority.
Exact match on date and status.
Exact match on date and status.

discrepancies.reasoning: RDCR (Event 2) explicitly states 'Diplomatic relations were reestablished on December 4, 1848' — this is an explicit institutional date for the reestablishment of relations. POCOM (Event 8) gives 1848-10-02 as the date Nathan Clifford presented credentials in Mexico City, which is a personnel/credential event. Per source priority rules, RDCR wins when it provides an explicit institutional date. The CSV uses the POCOM date (1848-10-02) and even notes 'Chiefs of Mission page for Mexico' as its source, but the correct date per RDCR should be 1848-12-04. The RDCR date refers to when President Polk accepted the Mexican envoy's credentials, formally reestablishing diplomatic relations. The CSV date should be updated to 1848-12-04.
